speaker: interviewee
text: --thing that I can safely say to anyone who has to face problems in the world. If you can save your sense of humor, or make some-- do some things which you would have done as a-- as a teenager in school, just-- just for the--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Continuing with Dr. Herbert Zipper. Talking to Sandy Jacobson, Los Angeles, California. In English, 9th of February, 1995. Tape number five.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father got you the exit visas. And where did you and your brother go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went first to Vienna. We had to go to Vienna. I mean, the order was to go from this-- from the train station directly to the head office of the Gestapo. And there, we had to start at bargaining for how many days they could let us be in Vienna, because we had so many things to, to, to bring in order until they finally let us out and taking away everything. I mean, if we had-- if we had known that [LAUGHS] this is the procedure, we could have gone earlier.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you stay in Vienna then?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed in Vienna [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 20 days, about.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from there, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you met your family there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We met the family. The whole family was there at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the first time you were reunited with the whole family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That was the first time. Didn't last long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I got a job in Manila.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why Manila? What was the appeal of Manila?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because Trudl was there. Look, there was many circumstances that, that I cannot explain. And the head of the Academy of Music and the head of the orchestra in Manila was a Viennese, whom I knew, and who suddenly died of a heart attack. Comparatively young. [INAUDIBLE], I think he was 48. He was about-- was much older than I. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he happened to accompany Trudl with the orchestra on her, her ballet on one-- on one whole series of compositions that I wrote for her. And she was very successful. That was just a few months before he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my name was known in Manila. And she was asked where I could be found. And she happened just to know that I-- because I, I sent her a telegram. And so I went to Manila. They offered me the job. There you and Trudl--

speaker: interviewee
text: And there-- together.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were together, and you were married there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had-- you were also arrested by the Japanese.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I made speeches in Manila.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of speeches?

speaker: interviewee
text: Attacking the Axis. You know what the Axis was. The Axis was the Berlin-Rome-Tokyo Axis. Attacked-- attacked them on, on cultural-- pieces of culture and mishandling. And I made speeches that, that appeared on the front page of newspapers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Japanese arrested you and took you to prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No, not to prison. They took me to police prison, where they-- what they called the enemy number one was, was assembled.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the Japanese different as jailers than the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, you see, it was not a normal jail. It was a police prison. And some people were shot. And some people were, I'm sure, mistreated. They never touched me. They interrogated me for, for a whole month every day. And-- But there was not that kind of manipulation that the Germans did. They were a-- always different. Also, there was one lieutenant-- Yamomoto was his name-- who was one of the high aristocratic families who himself was once badly, badly injured by the higher Japanese forces because he was too well educated. He spoke English very well. He spoke French quite well and played chess very well. So I played chess with him every day. And there I learned a great deal about Japanese society, which is another field which I'm now being asked a great deal about. Because I learned things from him that you don't read in books. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You see, this is entirely different. The Japanese fascism, even as it is held today, is completely different.

speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Helicopter.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --completely different from European one. The fascism is something that every person feels directly. It is a fascism of group pressure, not government pressure or some other pressure. It is group pressure and-- of which one is-- it's difficult or even impossible to escape. It's a very difficult-- different thing. It's very highly intelligent and not less insidious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did the Japanese release you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Release me? Because they didn't get-- they didn't get to any way, what they wanted me to do. Because I know the way how to tell them that I can't do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they want you to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They wanted me to reorganize the orchestra again and to use it for propaganda [? purposes. ?] Sure, that's what everybody does. And I told them as long-- I told them, as long as you keep me in here, I don't know. I cannot promise you. But you just promise. I said, no. No, that's against my conscience. I cannot promise you anything which I know-- I don't know that I can keep it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they let you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: So then they told me that I would be killed. I told them, I said, well, everybody has to die one day. You just do what you have to do. I do what I have to do. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So then they really wanted me to kill them in the end. I knew that when, when things become bad when, when the Americans landed. And later, I told my wife, and now we are going to move again. And nobody's going to know where we are going to stay. Because after they released you, then you were out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they watched me. They watched me. They watched me to find out that, that I tried to do all kinds of things that they want me to do. And they couldn't do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then once the Americans were on-- in, in the Philippines, then you felt that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I knew that they are going to-- anyone who was in that prison once they killed. Many, many of my friends were killed that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you, you became active, and you became kind of a spy yourself. Didn't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: You can call it that, yeah. You can call it that. That means I was asked by a group of, of Philippine-Chinese people, who-- especially the Chinese, who had-- who knew what, what the Japanese were, were doing in, in, in China. They knew about Nanjing. And they knew about those horrible things that had happened. So they were the most reliable ones. And they wanted to do whatever. They kept their radio going and asked me to help them to organize it and to get news, write the news for them, write what, what they should broadcast. And so I did all kinds of, of unsavory things that I felt I had to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw-- you heard that the Japanese were going to, to issue propaganda about Americans coming in. And so you got a hold of some money, some peso bills. And you were writing-- talk about the war. You were writing on money?

words.text: And so you got a hold of

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, that's what I did with my friend, Benito [? Legarda. ?] Sure, we-- it-- we printed these up one Sunday afternoon, on, on the Mickey-- what we called Mickey Mouse money, and dropped them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you write on the money?

speaker: interviewee
text: We wrote them that the Japanese are painting six of their bombers, with American insignias in order to bomb Manila to arouse their anger against the Americans of the population. And this-- they were-- they became a collector's item, these centavos that we had printed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many did you do?

speaker: interviewer
text: You wrote that-- hand wrote that on 10--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't wrote-- we didn't write. No, no. I found  a way-- found a way to, to, to print it. On a sten-- first we did it on a stencil. And then we used-- we used-- what do you call?

speaker: interviewer
text: A roller.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a roller. And dried it on a table in the office of [? Legarda. ?] We worked very hard on it. But we got the thousands done that could be easily read. And he dropped it on north, north Manila, that is, north of the Pasig River. And I dropped it on the Pasig. I made a hole in my pocket and dropped it on the streets and in markets and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you foiled the plan, didn't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the, the next day, they had an editorial in the newspaper, 'stupid American propaganda.' Of course, since everybody know-- knew it. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was also a funny story you tell about, you watched from the window of your house as a Japanese ammo truck was stuck coming up.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was funny. Oh, yeah. But it was an ammunition truck with, with Japanese driver and the-- an officer next to him. And they, they got stuck. And I don't think that-- you see, they were not-- they were not mechanics. They were just-- they could drive a truck. Then when the truck got stuck, they-- obviously the battery got off. So they started opening it up and hammering and so forth. And of course, immediately, a Philippine mechanic came there-- the Philippines have first-rate mechanics-- who said he's going to help them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he's going to help them. He did all kinds of things and gave them to  their hands and so forth. While he was doing it, the population gathered around. And, and it was immediately a big to do. There were dozens of people around it. And some young Filipinos jumped up, and they-- and started emptying the truck. Emptying the back of the truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: The back of the truck, all the ammunition. It disappeared into the darkness, gone. Gone was it. [LAUGHS] Finally, the mechanic said to them, battery. So he got a battery and put it in. The car started running. They were extremely thankful, bowed deeply, and drove off with an empty truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- as you said--

speaker: interviewee
text: Listen, my dear--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I'm just doing one more story about the boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: About the boy and the rug.

speaker: interviewee
text: The boy in the what?

speaker: interviewer
text: The boy that you wrapped up in the rug.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you want to see the rug?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we will see it. First, tell the story. And then  I'll let you go get the rug. And then I'll let you go after that.

words.text: And then  I'll let you go
And then I'll let you go after

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, was it was in the first days of the-- of liberation. Must have been about 50 years, three or four days ago. And in front of my-- of my apartment, my house-- actually, we occupied the whole house-- there was a boy lying, looking dead, practically, or near of it. It was in the evening. And we heard that he was breathing. See, in fact, he was breathing very loud. He had a hole here in the chest and a hole in the back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So it must have been a, a bullet that went through him where the heart is. But the heart was, was pumping nicely. But he was very weak and still, blood. He couldn't talk. So my friend and Lucio-- who was called Lucio at that time. He had a real different name, but he was with me on that group. We decided to take him. We have to, to save that boy. So I had took-- went upstairs and took one carpet of mine, one of those Chinese carpets, and we wrapped him in that. And carried him around. And for this next few weeks, we kept him as our mascot.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as we found out later when we could get him to the-- to the American doctor, who was next to the company, we find out that the bullet struck the ribs and went along the ribs and on this other side out, but never touched the inside. But the heart-- the lung was punctured. And therefore, he had a pneumothorax. That's why he was whistling out of his hole.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you took that boy with on every move, didn't you? Wrapped up in the rug, you kept him alive.

words.text: you took that boy with on every move,

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure. That's what you do. I mean, that's-- that is a very natural reaction. We kept him alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Zipper, at the end of this story, this incredible, extraordinary story, what is the message? What value is there in what you've learned of the suffering and deprivation that you've seen? What, what message do you give the person who's watched this video and who's said, what did you learn?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's very difficult to put into one sentence, my dear.

speaker: interviewer
text: You [INTERPOSING VOICES]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because there are too many things. You learn too many things. But the one important thing is that you learn through adversity. No, adversity's not the right word. Through real-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] through the real-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Let me think a little bit. It's difficult to put as a message. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The tragic thing is that the best in the human race comes forward when it is in real trouble. The humdrum of every day buries it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that sometimes we should be exposed, at least, by any of the artists-- of the art ways, to experience the real problems of humanity. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Unless we really experience them, these problems, we are not really human. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We become human when we find [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that we all actually face the same dangers, the same problems, and that we don't fool about-- fool ourselves that we are immune to them. You can see that when President Kennedy was shot. In the 72 hours afterwards, when the whole drama became really sunk into the-- to the whole nation, this nation became magnificent in every respect. If you recall-- you may not recall that radio and television, nothing that was trivial was broadcast. Suddenly the people listened to magnificent music, poetry, and whatnot. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because there was something really grievous happened to all of them. And this is again and again, I recall in life when this-- when the worst happens to us, the best in us comes forward. You see, after that Northridge earthquake, how many magnificent things happened. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So the lesson of my life is to think and find that something very, very troublesome and something even very dramatic happens every day. And that we just shouldn't look the other way, and should take part in it. That's it. Thank you very much. Thank you. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And go.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the Zipper family at home in Vienna. Walter, aged 13, is standing. Otto, age 1 and 1/2, then Hedy, who was then age 8, and Herbert in the middle, age 11. The photo was taken in 1915.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK. Stay back a little bit from it. Thanks. Speed.

words.text: back a little bit from it.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a publicity shot of Trudl-- who was to be Mrs. Herbert Zipper-- perched atop a telephone pole in Vienna, in the year 1929. In the background, you can see the spire of St. Stephen's.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I'm just going to widen out just a little bit, just to make sure I've got that whole picture in there. OK. Back, back, back, back. Sorry. And excellent. Lean out  a little bit. And go.

words.text: just going to widen out just a little bit,

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a passport photo of Herbert Zipper, taken in March, 1926.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Stand by. Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Trudl and Herbert Zipper shortly after they were married. Picture was taken in Manila in 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a picture taken on the journey to America. The captain of the Ship, that carried the Zippers to the US is holding their dachshund, Nitty. And the picture was taken by Trudl in San Francisco Harbor in 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have we named everyone in the picture?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, we haven't. Let's see, we've got-- the captain is holding the dog. And Dr. Zipper is on the right, camera right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Do we know who the other two guys are?

words.text: Do we know who the other two guys are?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're blocking it. You're totally blocking it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, that's good.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the deal. OK, Rolling. Go ahead. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold on a second.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the Zipper family in the US.

speaker: interviewee
text: See, I cannot read what it says.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the Zipper family in America. In the-- in the back row on the left is Herbert, his wife Trudl, his brother Otto. And in the front row, it's Mr. and Mrs. Zipper. The picture was taken in 1946, in March of 1946, in the US.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was a tiny thing.
